coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the production and photocatalytic efficacy of a Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO composite for the degradation of pharmaceutical contaminants, namely metronidazole and paracetamol, under visible light and sunlight. The catalyst was produced via a hydrothermal process and studied using XRD, SEM, TEM, FTIR, UV–Vis DRS, and EIS techniques. The findings demonstrate substantial photocatalytic efficacy, attaining 99 % degradation of metronidazole and paracetamol in 100 and 180 min, respectively, under visible light. Optimal photocatalytic conditions were determined, encompassing pollutant concentration, catalyst dosage, and pH. Mechanistic investigations reveal hydroxyl radicals as the principal active species, augmented by superoxide anions and holes. The catalyst exhibited strong reusability across five cycles with negligible efficiency decline. The application in actual water source demonstrated significant degradation efficiencies, highlighting its potential for environmental restoration.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the production and photocatalytic efficacy of a Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO composite for the degradation of pharmaceutical contaminants, namely metronidazole and paracetamol, under visible light and sunlight. The catalyst was produced via a hydrothermal process and studied using XRD, SEM, TEM, FTIR, UV–Vis DRS, and EIS techniques. The findings demonstrate substantial photocatalytic efficacy, attaining 99 % degradation of metronidazole and paracetamol in 100 and 180 min, respectively, under visible light. Optimal photocatalytic conditions were determined, encompassing pollutant concentration, catalyst dosage, and pH. Mechanistic investigations reveal hydroxyl radicals as the principal active species, augmented by superoxide anions and holes. The catalyst exhibited strong reusability across five cycles with negligible efficiency decline. The application in actual water source demonstrated significant degradation efficiencies, highlighting its potential for environmental restoration.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceuticals, especially antibiotics, have significantly transformed modern medicine by effectively combating bacterial infections and saving innumerable lives. However, their extensive use and improper disposal have emerged as a pressing environmental issue [1–6]. These compounds are now categorized as micropollutants frequently detected across diverse ecosystems, including surface and groundwater, soil, and sediments. Antibiotics, in particular, are persistent contaminants that retain their activity even after being excreted by humans or animals. They enter the environment through wastewater treatment plants, agricultural runoff, and industrial effluents [7–9]. The widespread presence of antibiotics in the environment poses significant dangers. These drugs apply selection pressure on microbial populations, promoting the establishment and expansion of antibiotic-resistant bacteria (ARB) and antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) [10–13]. This process presents a twofold danger to ecological integrity and human health since resistant bacteria can propagate via aquatic systems and food chains. The growing prevalence of pharmaceutical chemicals in wastewater effluents intensifies these dangers. Despite their widespread application, antibiotic residues have been identified in multiple aquatic ecosystems at approximately 1–100 ng/L [9,14]. The presence of antibiotics in the environment can result in antimicrobial resistance among bacteria. Antibiotics exert selective pressure that enables resistant bacteria to endure and proliferate. These resistant bacteria can disseminate via water systems, soil, and food, threatening ecosystems and human health. Consequently, regulating antibiotic contamination is crucial to avert the emergence of resistance strains and safeguard public health [15–17].

Metronidazole is a frequently prescribed antibiotic and antiprotozoal agent utilized for illnesses induced by anaerobic bacteria and certain parasites, including bacterial vaginosis and Clostridium difficile. It disrupts these microbes’DNA, inhibiting their growth and viability [18,19]. Metronidazole's extensive utilization often leads to its presence in wastewater systems, prompting worries regarding its environmental impact. Metronidazole poses environmental challenges due to its high persistence and resistance to conventional wastewater treatment processes. Its accumulation in aquatic settings can adversely affect aquatic organisms, disturb microbial populations, and foster antibiotic resistance [20–23]. Paracetamol, or acetaminophen, is a commonly utilized over-the-counter analgesic and antipyretic medicine. It is among the most widely utilized substances worldwide, available in multiple formulations, including tablets, syrups, and combination medicines [24,25]. Paracetamol, owing to its extensive utilization, significantly contaminates the environment via pharmaceutical waste, incorrect disposal, and untreated wastewater [26–29]. Paracetamol presents environmental problems due to its persistence and probable toxicity. It can adversely affect aquatic organisms by interfering with their biological functions and deteriorating water quality. Moreover, its presence in aquatic systems can modify microbial communities and generate hazardous byproducts during incomplete breakdown, such as p-aminophenol, which is detrimental to aquatic organisms. Decomposing paracetamol in the environment is crucial to avert prolonged ecological harm, safeguard aquatic habitats, and mitigate the dangers of bioaccumulation and toxicity [30–36].

Photocatalysis is a sophisticated oxidation process wherein a photocatalyst, usually a semiconductor such as titanium dioxide (TiO2) or zinc oxide (ZnO), enhances a chemical reaction when exposed to light irradiation [37–43,95,97]. Upon exposure to light, typically in the ultraviolet (UV) or visible spectrum, the photocatalyst absorbs photons, producing electron-hole pairs that trigger a sequence of processes. These reactions generate highly reactive species, including hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anions, capable of degrading contaminants into less hazardous or biodegradable compounds. The necessity for photocatalysis stems from its efficacy in tackling environmental issues, especially the elimination of persistent organic pollutants (POPs) from water and air. Numerous traditional wastewater treatment methods fail to fully breakdown pharmaceutical compounds, dyes, insecticides, and other hazardous pollutants [100–103]. Photocatalysis provides a sustainable and effective solution that functions in mild conditions, employs light as an energy source, and eschews toxic chemicals, thus presenting an eco-friendly method for environmental cleanup. The photocatalytic degradation process commences with the photocatalyst's absorption of light, which excites electrons from the valence band to the conduction band, forming electron-hole pairs. Electrons interact with oxygen molecules to generate reactive oxygen species such as superoxide anions, whereas holes oxidize water or hydroxide ions to get hydroxyl radicals. These reactive species oxidize and decompose organic contaminants into smaller, non-toxic molecules such as carbon dioxide, water, or simple ions, successfully mitigating environmental contamination [44–49,74,96]. Zinc oxide (ZnO) is a wide-bandgap semiconductor recognized for its superior photocatalytic characteristics when exposed to ultraviolet (UV) radiation. It is extensively utilized in photocatalysis owing to its robust oxidative potential, exceptional chemical stability, and capacity to produce reactive oxygen species. ZnO functions as a photocatalyst by absorbing ultraviolet light, which promotes electrons from the valence band to the conduction band, forming positively charged holes. Electron-hole couples facilitate the generation of reactive species, including hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anions. These highly reactive species oxidize and decompose contaminants, reducing them to smaller, non-toxic molecules such as water and carbon dioxide. ZnO's effectiveness availability and eco-friendliness render it a significant material for mitigating environmental pollution via photocatalytic processes [50–53]. Ti3C2 MXene is a two-dimensional nanomaterial within the MXene family, distinguished by its excellent conductivity, large surface area, and adaptable surface chemistry. It consists of titanium and carbon layers with surface terminations such as –OH, –O, or –F, which enhance its reactivity. Ti3C2 MXene is increasingly utilized in photocatalysis due to its ability to improve charge separation, enhance light absorption, and facilitate the transfer of photogenerated electrons. In photocatalytic processes, Ti3C2 MXene acts as an electron acceptor or co-catalyst, reducing the recombination of electron-hole pairs and boosting the production of reactive species such as hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anions. These species efficiently degrade pollutants by oxidizing them into smaller, less harmful molecules, making Ti3C2 MXene a promising material for effective and sustainable environmental remediation [54–59,91–94]. Carbon dots (CDs) are a category of carbon-based nanomaterials, typically measuring less than 10 nm, recognized for their distinctive optical properties, superior biocompatibility, and exceptional electron transfer efficiency. They are increasingly utilized in photocatalysis because they enhance light absorption across a broad spectrum, promote charge separation, and function as both electron donors and acceptors. When applied in photocatalytic processes, carbon dots capture light energy and transfer photogenerated electrons to semiconductor materials, thereby reducing electron-hole recombination, the previous study shows the CDs has effective for degradation various pollutants (Table 1 ). Furthermore, CDs can generate reactive oxygen species, including hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anions, which efficiently decompose contaminants by oxidizing them into less hazardous substances. Carbon dots are highly desirable for sustainable environmental applications due to their eco-friendliness, tunable surface functionalities, and high catalytic effectiveness [60–66].

This research utilized direct hydrothermal methods to synthesize Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO catalyst powder. The optical, morphological, and structural properties were analyzed, and the findings were compared with previous studies, as summarized in Table 2 .

All chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade, Ti-metal powder (99 %), Al-metal powder (99 %), graphite (99 %), were purchased from HiMedia Laboratories, USA. Zinc Oxide (99 %) and hydrofluoric acid, (48 %) were purchased from Smart Science, Thailand.

Pure titanium, aluminum, and graphite in a ratio of 3:1:2 was prepared and placed into an aluminum crucible (dimensions of 110 mm in height and 60 mm in inner diameter). The vertical tube furnace was heated to a temperature of 1200 °C and held at that level for 2 h. Afterward, the reactor began a spontaneous cooling process until it reached room temperature (RT) around 25 °C. Subsequently, the sample underwent purification by rinsing it with ultrasonicated pure water. The highest level of purity for MAX was achieved with desiccation at a temperature of 80 °C using an electric oven. In order to produce Ti3C2 MXene, the Ti3AlC2 MAX material was combined with a 49 % HF solution and subjected to continuous stirring for 48 h. The Ti3AlC2 MAX was added to the HF solution gradually; The Mxene was thoroughly washed with deionized water until the solution reached a pH of 6, and the powder was dried at 80 °C for 24 h [79,80].

Raw mango peels were procured from the Khon Kaen fresh market during November and December 2024. The peels were carefully washed several times with deionized (DI) water and subsequently dried in an oven at 80 °C for 24 h. Once dried, the mango peels were ground into a fine powder. Following the procedure outlined by Nugroho et al., 1 g of mango peel powder was combined with 40 mL of DI water and placed in a hydrothermal Teflon vessel. The vessel was then heated in an oven at 180 °C for 4 h. After the synthesis was completed, the carbon dots solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min at room temperature. The resulting supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm nylon syringe filter, and the final carbon dots solution (CDs) was stored in a refrigerator for further use [60–62].

One gram of Ti3C2 MXene and 1 g of ZnO were combined with 40 mL of carbon dots (CDs), followed by 100 mL of methanol. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h to ensure thorough blending. After mixing, the solution was transferred to a hydrothermal vessel and subjected to synthesis at 180 °C for 4 h. The resulting product was dried at 60 °C for 12 h and subsequently ground into a fine powder. Finally, the powder was calcined at 400 °C for 4 h [60,64].

The materials were analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (SHIMADZU XRD-6000, Japan) equipped with a Cu Kα X-ray source, operating at a wavelength of 1.5406 Å. The chemical compositions were examined through energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis and scanning electron microscopy (SEM-EDX; JSM-5600 JEOL, Japan). The sample morphologies were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (TEM: Hitachi HT7700, operated at 100 kV; HRTEM: Hitachi H9500, operated at 300 kV, Japan). Diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were obtained using a UV–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU UV-2600, Japan) over a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The bandgap energies of the photocatalysts were calculated using the modified Kubelka-Munk function derived from the UV–Vis DRS data. The resistivity of the samples was measured using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with a Zahner electrochemical analyzer from Germany. Photocurrent measurements were carried out using an SLS 301-stabilized tabletop tungsten-halogen light source fitted with a 150 W halogen bulb.

The photodegradation efficiency of various pollutants in the samples was evaluated under visible light. 50 mg of the synthesized catalyst materials were added to 50 mL of a 10 ppm solution containing different pollutants (Paracetamol and Metronidazole). The solution was left in the dark for 60 min to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Following this, it was irradiated from above while stirring at room temperature. At predetermined intervals, 5.0 mL of the solution was sampled to measure the concentrations of Paracetamol and Metronidazole using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, with absorbance recorded at 243 nm for Paracetamol and 258 nm for Metronidazole. The optimal conditions for photodegradation using the Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO catalyst were investigated by varying pH, pollutant concentrations, catalyst dosages, and light intensities. A comparison between sunlight and visible light was also conducted, with sunlight tests performed on the rooftop of the Faculty of Science, SC 04 (latitude 16°28′33.7″N and longitude 102°49′26.2″E) on January 12, 2025. The role of active species in the photodegradation process was explored using scavenger experiments with benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA), and potassium iodide (KI) to target O2 −, ∙OH, and h+ species, respectively. Furthermore, the catalyst's application in treating wastewater samples was studied using Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO. The wastewater sample was collected from Sritan Lake, Khon Kaen University (latitude 16.443272′N and longitude 102.816108′E), Plastic Lake, Khon Kaen University (latitude 16.473214′N and longitude 102.819484′E), and Tap Water from Department of Integrated Science Lab, SC01, Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University (latitude 16.475639 ′N, and longitude 102.823979′E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and elemental analysis of catalyst powder
ce:section.ce:para:
The crystal structure of the different catalyst powders was examined using XRD analysis, as illustrated in Fig. 1 (a). The diffraction patterns validate the existence of ZnO and Ti3C2 within the Ti3C2Mxene/CDs/ZnO composite. The distinctive peaks, comprising (002) for Ti3C2, (101) and (110) for ZnO, and (110) for Ti3C2, were recognized. The mean crystal size was determined to be 18.95 nm, as shown in Table 3 , utilizing the Scherrer equation,
(1) D = (kλ/β cos θ)

The results demonstrate that the Al layers were successfully eliminated from the parent MAX phase, leading to the synthesis of Ti3C2MXene, in agreement with a previous study. Furthermore, minor TiO2 peaks, ascribed to low-index planes, were detected (JCPDS ZnO: PDF#36–1451, JCPDS TiO2: PDF#21–1272).

FTIR results in Fig. 1(b) show typical stretching vibration absorption peaks, including those corresponding to the hydroxyl (–OH) functional group at 3455 cm−1, CO at 1740 cm−1, C–F at 1073 cm−1, Ti–C stretching at 694 cm−1, Ti–O stretching at 590 cm−1, and ZnO bond at 435 cm−1 [76,77]

The bandgap energy was analyzed using UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS); the results are illustrated in Fig. 1(c). The Kubelka-Munk formula was employed to ascertain the correlation between energy (Eg) and optical absorption. Fig. 1(c) illustrates the band gap energies of Ti3C2MXene, Ti3C2MXene/ZnO, Ti3C2MXene/CDs, and Ti3C2MXene/CDs/ZnO, with projected values of 3.31, 3.01, 3.04, and 2.89 eV, respectively. A low bandgap is beneficial for photocatalysts because it allows the material to absorb a broader range of solar radiation, including visible and near-infrared light, enhancing its photocatalytic efficiency. By capturing a broader spectrum of sunlight, low-bandgap materials generate a more significant number of electron-hole pairs upon illumination, hence improving the efficiency of photochemical processes such as pollutant degradation, hydrogen production, or CO2 reduction. Furthermore, the ability to absorb low-energy photons improves the effectiveness of these photocatalysts in practical, low-light settings, enhancing their performance in sustainable energy and environmental applications [78–80]. Fig. 1(d) presents the Mott-Schottky plots for various powder-form catalysts. This analysis is a vital tool in catalytic research, as it reveals the flat-band potential (Efb) and charge carrier density of a catalyst, providing valuable insights into its electronic properties and interfacial charge transfer mechanisms, which are crucial for optimizing photocatalytic and electrocatalytic performance. The Mott-Schottky plots for Ti3C2 MXene, Ti3C2 MXene/ZnO, Ti3C2 MXene/CDs, and Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO were obtained by analyzing their behavior at different applied potentials. All nanorod arrays exhibited positive curvature slopes, confirming their n-type semiconductor characteristics. The Efb values for Ti3C2 MXene, Ti3C2 MXene/ZnO, Ti3C2 MXene/CDs, and Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO were determined as 0.06, 0.07, 0.01, and −0.05 V, respectively. Notably, the Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO electrodes demonstrated the highest photocatalytic efficiency for hydrogen generation in photoelectrochemical cells, as evidenced by their most negative Efb value (−0.05 V). This negative Efb reflects a strong reduction potential and effective charge separation, significantly enhancing the material's performance in photoredox reactions under light irradiation.

The particular surface areas of the synthesized samples were determined using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurements. As illustrated in Fig. 1(e), Ti3C2MXene exhibits a type III isotherm, indicating its classification as a mesoporous material. Notably, the surface area of pristine Ti3C2 MXene increases significantly upon hybridization with CDs and ZnO. The calculated specific surface areas for Ti3C2MXene/CDs/ZnO, Ti3C2MXene/CDs, Ti3C2MXene/ZnO, and Ti3C2MXene are 1.37, 2.32, 2.16, and 2.86 m2/g, respectively. This substantial enhancement in specific surface area is highly beneficial for improving photocatalytic activity by offering additional active sites, enhancing light absorption, promoting charge separation, permitting effective mass transfer, and amplifying catalyst-substrate interaction. The Electrocatalytic activity of catalyst powder has been studied by EIS, as shown in the result in Fig. 1(f). The radius of the arc on the EIS Nyquist plot for Ti3C2MXene/CDs/ZnO is lowest than other catalyst powders, Ti3C2MXene/CDs, Ti3C2MXene/ZnO, and Ti3C2MXene, respectively. The heterojunction formed between Ti3C2 MXene, CDs, and ZnO promotes the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The CDs act as a bridge, allowing electrons to transfer efficiently from the conduction band of ZnO to Ti3C2 MXene. This combination of mechanisms leads to significantly improved photocatalytic efficiency, making Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO highly effective for applications such as hydrogen generation or pollutant degradation in photoelectrochemical systems [98,99]. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) plot also has been studied in this research and showed in Fig. 1(g), which evaluates the electrochemical performance of materials; in this graph, the Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO electrode exhibits the highest current density, indicating enhanced charge storage capacity and catalytic activity, attributed to the synergistic effects of the heterojunction between Ti3C2 MXene, CDs, and ZnO, which facilitates efficient electron transfer, reduces recombination, and promotes redox reactions at the electrode-electrolyte interface.

The morphology of the catalyst powders has been analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. 2 (a) illustrates the morphological characteristics under TEM of Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO. The TEM image illustrates a Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO composite exhibiting unique structural features. The Ti3C2 MXene is characterised by its layered, sheet-like structures with significant contrast, serving as the principal framework. Carbon dots (CDs) manifest as diminutive, black, spherical structures, roughly 2.3 nm in size, dispersed throughout the MXene layers. ZnO nanoparticles, characterised by their bigger and irregular morphology, are either incorporated in or affixed to the MXene sheets, with sizes ranging from 40 nm. The uniform distribution of CDs and ZnO signifies effective composite synthesis. Fig. 2(b) illustrates the outcome obtained using SEM. The SEM image depicts the surface morphology of a Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO composite. The MXene layers display a distinct lamellar, sheet-like configuration, characterised by irregular surfaces and loosely aggregated flakes. In contrast, the elemental analysis using EDX mapping verifies the presence of titanium (Ti) at 20.5 wt%, carbon (C) at 36.1 wt%, oxygen (O) at 10.5 wt%, zinc (Zn) at 29.1 wt%, and aluminium (Al) at 3.7 wt%, confirming the effective incorporation of ZnO and CDs into the Ti3C2 MXene matrix. The interconnected architecture identified in the SEM aligns with the TEM results, wherein ZnO nanoparticles were discovered embedded within the Ti3C2 MXene layers, while CDs were dispersed across the surface. To validate the EDX findings, XPS analysis was conducted on the Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO composite, with the results illustrated in Fig. 2(c)–2(g). Fig. 2(c) displays the XPS survey spectrum of the composite, revealing characteristic peaks for Zn2p, O1s, Ti2p, and C1s, confirming the incorporation of zinc, oxygen, titanium, and carbon within the composite. Fig. 2(d) focuses on the Zn2p region, where two peaks are observed at 1021.5 and 1045.3 eV, corresponding to Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2, respectively. These binding energies indicate Zn2+ ions, which suggest the presence of ZnO nanoparticles in the composite. Fig. 2(e) presents the O1s spectrum, which, upon deconvolution, reveals three peaks at 529.4, 530.1, and 531.4 eV. The peak at 529.4 eV is assigned to lattice oxygen in the ZnO structure, the peak at 530.1 eV corresponds to oxygen in CO bonds, and the peak at 531.4 eV is attributed to oxygen in hydroxyl (O–H) groups, highlighting the presence of functional groups on the surface of the composite. Fig. 2(f) illustrates the Ti2p spectrum, where deconvolution reveals two peaks at 458.7 and 464.2 eV, attributed to Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2, respectively. These peaks are consistent with titanium in the Ti–C bonds of the MXene framework, confirming its structural integrity. Finally, Fig. 2(g) depicts the deconvoluted C1s spectrum, showing 284.2 and 283.5 eV peaks. The peak at 284.2 eV corresponds to C–C and is associated with carbon dots (CDs),which are a signature of the graphitic structure or sp2 [78–80].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation of pollutants by various conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The photodegradation performance of various catalyst powder Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO, Ti3C2 MXene/CDs, Ti3C2 MXene/ZnO, Ti3C2 MXene, was investigated for two pharmaceutical pollutants metronidazole and paracetamol. Fig. 3 showed a significant reduction in concentration upon exposure to visible light. Fig. 3(a) and (b) show the result of the photodegradation of metronidazole; initially, in the dark, there was negligible degradation, confirming the photo-dependent nature of the process. Once the light was turned on, a steep decline in metronidazole concentration was observed, indicating rapid photodegradation; the result shows that Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO, the optimum condition compared to other catalyst pollutant, with % degradation until 99 % after 100 min under visible light, The kinetic analysis in Fig. 3(b), revealed a linear relationship between ln(C0/C) and time, consistent with pseudo-first-order kinetics of Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO with R2 = 0.97 and K = 0.068 min−1, respectively. That indicated the slope for the Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO system compared to other tested photocatalysts highlights its superior photocatalytic activity. Similarly, for paracetamol (Fig. 3(c) and (d), the concentration remained stable in the dark but decreased markedly upon light exposure, indicating the effectiveness of the photocatalyst. Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO can degrade the paracetamol 99 % after 180 min, with R2 = 0.99, K = 0.009 min−1. However, the degradation rate was slower than metronidazole, which may be attributed to differences in molecular structure and binding affinity. The enhanced photocatalytic efficiency of Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO can be attributed to the synergistic effects between its components. Ti3C2 MXene provides a large surface area and facilitates charge separation, while CDs and ZnO enhance visible-light absorption and reactive species generation.

After establishing the optimal conditions, numerous parameters of photodegradation were studied, including different concentrations of pollutants, varying concentrations of catalyst powder, diverse pH levels, and multiple light sources. Different concentrations of metronidazole and paracetamol pollutants were prepared at 5, 10, and 20 ppm, as illustrated in Fig. 4 (a) and (b). The Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO catalyst powder remained effective for the degradation of metronidazole and paracetamol at a concentration of 20 ppm after 180 min under visible light, achieving degradation rates of 99.4 % and 97.1 %, respectively. Fig. 4(a) and (b) illustrate the optimal concentration of 5 ppm, attributed to reduced competition among pollutant molecules for the active sites on the photocatalyst surface. A lower concentration of pollutants results in decreased turbidity, allowing greater light penetration to reach the photocatalyst. This facilitates the production of reactive species, including hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anions, which propel the breakdown process [81,82]. Fig. 4(c) and (d) illustrate the outcomes of varying concentrations of the catalyst powder Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO (25, 50, and 75 mg). The highest concentration (75 mg) of Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO demonstrated optimal conditions for the degradation of various pollutants; metronidazole was completely degraded after 60 min, while paracetamol was degraded after 40 min. The highest concentration of catalyst powder yields superior results due to the increased presence of photocatalyst particles, which generates a bigger number of photoexcited electrons and holes under light irradiation, indicating a larger quantity of photocatalyst particles in the solution. This expands the total surface area accessible for the adsorption of pollutant molecules, facilitating increased pollutant-catalyst interactions and, improving the degradation process [83,84]. Fig. 4(e) and (f) show the result of the effect of various pH (3, 5, 7, 9, and 11) conditions in the degradation of metronidazole and paracetamol by Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO. The photodegradation of metronidazole and paracetamol utilizing Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO composites exhibits distinct pH dependencies attributable to differences in the surface charge of the catalysts, the characteristics of the pollutants, and the photocatalytic pathways involved. The photodegradation of metronidazole using Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO was most efficient at pH 9 and least efficient at pH 3, attributed to the impact of pH on catalyst performance, pollutant adsorption, and the production of reactive species. At pH 9, the catalyst's surface charge promoted pollutant adsorption, and the alkaline conditions increased hydroxyl radical (•OH) generation while reducing electron-hole recombination, leading to maximum degradation efficiency. On the other hand, at pH 3, the acidic environment induced a positive charge on the catalyst surface, resulting in repulsion from metronidazole molecules and decreased adsorption. Moreover, the surplus H+ ions in acidic environments functioned as scavengers for reactive species, markedly diminishing the photodegradation effectiveness [85,86]. The photodegradation of paracetamol utilizing Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO was most effective at pH 7 and least effective at pH 11. The photodegradation of paracetamol is most effective at pH 7 (neutral), where the catalyst's surface charge and the pollutant's interactions are optimally balanced, resulting in enhanced adsorption and reactive oxygen species formation. At pH 11 (alkaline), the catalyst surface becomes excessively negative, diminishing paracetamol adsorption and impeding the photocatalytic activity. The effects of various visible light sources (15, 30, and 75 W) on the photodegradation of metronidazole and paracetamol using Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO have been examined, as illustrated in Fig. 4(g) and (h). The findings indicate that the visible lamp's highest wattage (75W) provides the optimal conditions for both pollutants, as increased light intensity enhances the excitation of electrons in the photocatalyst. This results in more effective production of electron-hole pairs, an essential process in the creation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) like hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide anions (O2−), which are important for the degradation of pollutants. The increased energy input enhances the activation of the catalyst's surface, facilitating more efficient interactions with the target pollutant [87,88].

The degradation of various pollutants (metronidazole and paracetamol) was compared by sunlight and visible light, it was shown in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5(a) and (b) show the optimum conditions of degradation metronidazole, visible light and sunlight can degrade 99 % after 80 and 100 min with R2 = 0.98, 0.96 and K = 0.068, 0.028 min−1, respectively. Fig. 5(c) and (d) show the optimum conditions of degradation paracetamol, visible light and sunlight can degrade 99 % after 100 min with R2 = 0.94, 0.97 and K = 0.021, 0.028 min−1, respectively. While sunlight provides a wider range of wavelengths, the photocatalyst demonstrated enhanced degradation efficiency when exposed to visible light. This indicates that the process occurring under visible light is influenced by the ideal spectral alignment with the catalyst's absorption spectrum, resulting in enhanced generation of electron-hole pairs. Conversely, in the presence of sunlight, various wavelengths might trigger less efficient pathways or lead to the emergence of competing processes. Consequently, the mechanisms of degradation vary not only based on the range of light but also on the efficiency with which the catalyst harnesses particular wavelengths [102–105]. After the first cycle of degradation, the powder was washed with DI water several times and dried in the oven, and used for the next cycle until 5th cycle, the results are shown in Fig. 5(e) and (f). Fig. 5(e) shows the result under the visible light, after 5th cycle, the %degradation of metronidazole decrease to 97 % in the 4th cycles, and increase back to 99 % in the 5th cycle of recycle, The reduction in efficiency during the 4th cycle (97 %) and the subsequent recovery in the 5th cycle (99 %) possibly stem from transient surface fouling or deactivation by intermediates, followed by self-cleaning or reactivation of active sites under the high-energy light source, illustrating the catalyst's resilience and the %degradation of paracetamol has slightly decrease after 5th cycle become 98 %. Fig. 5(f) shows the result under the sunlight, after 5th cycle the %degradation of metronidazole and paracetamol slightly decrease to 98 % in the 5th cycles, for confirmation the stability of catalyst powder Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO after 5th cycle, they catalyst powder was analyzed by using CV test and FTIR, it's shows in Fig. 6 . The result depicts the photocatalytic efficacy and mechanistic understanding of the Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO composite under various conditions and for distinct contaminants (metronidazole and paracetamol). Fig. 6(a) illustrates the current-potential relationship (CV curve), emphasizing alterations in electronic characteristics before and after photocatalytic degradation. Following the degradation of metronidazole, a minor increase in current is noted, indicating enhanced charge transfer efficiency. The current response for paracetamol exhibits a significant decline, suggesting potential deactivation of the surface or alterations resulting from its interaction with the pollutant. In light of these variations, the overall current profiles observed before and after the photocatalytic reactions exhibit only minor differences, indicating that the nanocomposites sustain commendable electrochemical stability throughout the process. Fig. 6(b), an FTIR spectrum, verifies chemical structure alterations before and after photocatalysis. Prominent peaks associated with functional groups (e.g., O–H stretching, CO stretching, and C–F bonds) are recognized. Following degradation, the peaks corresponding to contaminants decrease, signifying the effective disintegration of molecular structures. The retention of significant catalyst peaks (e.g., Ti–C vibrations) indicates the stability of the composite. Fig. 6(c) and (d) in the absence of scavengers, the composite attains above 99 % efficiency for both contaminants. Incorporating scavengers (KI for holes, IPA for hydroxyl radicals, and BQ for superoxide radicals) elucidates the primary reactive oxygen species responsible for degradation. The substantial decrease in efficiency with IPA and BQ for both pollutants indicates that hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide anions (O2−) are the principal active species, whereas KI exhibits a minor effect, suggesting a diminished function for holes. to explore the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) boundaries of constituents within the composite, the results of determining the potential of CB and VB edges of Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO are shown in Table 4 and Fig. 7 . Table 4 illustrates that this method can identify possible sites for H2O/O2 and elucidate the mechanism of charge carrier transfer in Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO nanocomposites. The standard redox potential of the H2O/O2 couple is 1.23 V about the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE). The hydroxyl radical (OH•) exhibits a standard redox potential of 2.3V in the presence of water (H2O) when referenced against the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE). The electrochemical potentials of Ti3C2 MXene, Ti3C2 MXene/CDs, Ti3C2 MXene/ZnO, and Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO about the standard hydrogen electrode (NHE) are presented in Table 4. The Ecb/V against NHE values for Ti3C2 MXene, Ti3C2 MXene/CDs, Ti3C2 MXene/ZnO, and Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO were 0.166, 0.226, 0.286, and 0.276, respectively. The Ecb/V values for Ti3C2 MXene, Ti3C2 MXene/CDs, Ti3C2 MXene/ZnO, and Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO were 2.507, 3.207, 3.237, and 3.087, respectively. The following prediction equation encapsulates the active oxidation compounds generated throughout the process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of photodegradation metronidazole and paracetamol in waste water
ce:section.ce:para:
Table 5 displays the percentage recovery of metronidazole and paracetamol from several water matrices, specifically Sritan Lake (1 and 2), Plastic Lake (1 and 2), and Tap Water (1 and 2), at spiking doses of 5, 10, 15, and 20 mg/L. The recovery values, presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD) from five replicates (n = 5), are an essential preliminary measure in verifying the analytical approach before utilizing photocatalysis for pollutant degradation. These recovery studies validate the method's reliability, accuracy, and repeatability in quantifying the initial amounts of contaminants in intricate environmental samples.
(2) % recovery = [Cfound / Cadded] x 100,

The outcomes of this computation are presented in Table 5. In Sritan Lake 1, which contained metronidazole, the recovery percentages varied from 99.5 to 113.1 %. The maximum recovery (113.1 %) occurred at a spiking concentration of 10 mg/L, indicating a minor over-recovery potentially attributable to matrix effects, like organic or inorganic interference in the lake water. At the lowest dose of 5 mg/L, the recovery was 101.4 %, whereas at 20 mg/L, it was 99.5 %, demonstrating negligible variation from the optimal 100 % across the examined range. The recovery percentages in Sritan Lake 2, which included paracetamol, varied from 102.3 to 109.5 %. The maximum recovery (109.5 %) was observed at 20 mg/L, whilst the minimum (102.3 %) was recorded at 15 mg/L. The results demonstrate the method's resilience, while minor matrix effects in Sritan Lake 2 may amplify signal intensities. Plastic Lake 1 and 2, indicative of water retained in artificial settings, demonstrated elevated precision and accuracy. In Plastic Lake 1, the recoveries of metronidazole varied from 99.3 % to 105.7 %, exhibiting uniformity across all concentrations. In Plastic Lake 2, the recoveries of paracetamol ranged from 99.7 % to 102.1 %, indicating no interference from any leachates or pollutants derived from plastic storage. These results validate the method's usefulness in synthetic aquatic environments. The recovery percentages were remarkably constant in Tap Water 1 and 2, which represent cleaner matrices. In Tap Water 1 (metronidazole), recoveries varied from 99.3 % to 100.3 %, whereas in Tap Water 2 (paracetamol), recoveries ranged from 99.4 % to 100.6 %. The results indicate minimal interference in the pure water samples, reinforcing the method's robustness under uncomplicated matrix circumstances. Prior to implementing photocatalysis, it is imperative to conduct recovery studies to ensure the precise quantification of starting pollutant concentrations in environmental samples. The recovery data confirms that the analytical approach can consistently quantify drug concentrations in various complex matrices, including potential interfering chemicals. The observed recoveries, primarily within the acceptable range of 99–113 %, validate the analytical method's reliability and appropriateness for various water matrices. Consequently, the recovery data is a fundamental validation, guaranteeing the reliability of ensuing photocatalytic performance assessments. Fig. 8 illustrates the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of Metronidazole and Paracetamol in diverse water sources, including Sritan Lake, Plastic Lake, and Tap Water, as well as the involvement of different reactive species scavengers in influencing the degradation pathways. These findings are essential for evaluating the universality of the photocatalytic system and identifying the predominant reactive species involved in pollutant degradation. Fig. 8(a) and (b) present bar graphs that compare the % degradation of metronidazole and paracetamol across three water matrices. The degradation efficiencies for metronidazole are consistently elevated, surpassing 98 % across all matrices. The water from Sritan Lake exhibits marginally more degradation at 99 % compared to Plastic Lake and Tap Water, which show degradation rates of 97 % and 98 %, respectively. This slight variation can be ascribed to the elevated organic or inorganic load in natural lake water, which may compete for reactive species or impede photocatalysis. The degradation efficiencies for Paracetamol are virtually uniform across all matrices, with Sritan Lake at 99 %, Plastic Lake at 97 %, and Tap Water at 97 %. The elevated efficiency across multiple matrices illustrates the photocatalyst's resilience in managing various environmental difficulties. Fig. 8(c) and (d) elucidate the function of reactive species in the degradation of metronidazole and paracetamol in Sritan Lake water. In the absence of scavengers, the degradation efficiency is 99 %, signifying the complete functionality of the photocatalyst. Incorporating of potassium iodide (KI), a hole (h+) scavenger, marginally decreases the efficiency to 98 %, indicating that holes are pivotal in the degrading process. Isopropanol (IPA), a hydroxyl radical (•OH) scavenger, diminishes the degradation efficiency to 89 %, underscoring the significance of hydroxyl radicals in the photocatalytic process. Benzoquinone (BQ), a scavenger of superoxide radicals (O2 −•), demonstrates the lowest effectiveness at 67 %, suggesting a significant although secondary role for superoxide radicals. A comparable tendency is noted in the breakdown of Paracetamol in Sritan Lake. The lack of scavengers results in nearly total deterioration (99 %). The effectiveness diminishes by little to 97 % with KI, underscoring the significance of holes. IPA diminishes efficiency to 84 %, highlighting the preeminence of hydroxyl radicals. BQ reduces the efficiency to 59 %, corroborating the participation of superoxide radicals. The results collectively indicate that the photocatalyst demonstrates superior degrading efficacy for metronidazole and paracetamol across various water sources, implying its potential applicability in real-world wastewater treatment scenarios. Moreover, the scavenger experiments yield essential mechanistic insights, identifying hydroxyl radicals as the principal reactive species, while holes and superoxide radicals serve as auxiliary contributors. This mechanistic comprehension is essential for developing photocatalytic devices and improving their efficiency in practical applications. The equations below delineate the active oxidation chemicals produced during the magnetic photocatalyst process:
(3) Ti3C2 MXene + CDs + ZnO + hv → Ti3C2 MXene + CDs + ZnO + e− + h+
(4) h+ + H2O → OH + H−
(5) h+ + OH- → OH
(6) e− + O2 → O2
(7) O2 + H+ → HO2
(8) O2 / e− / OH + Pollutant → Degradation Product

This research effectively produced and analyzed a Ti3C2 MXene/CDs/ZnO composite exhibiting remarkable photocatalytic characteristics. The catalyst exhibited exceptional efficacy in degrading pharmaceutical contaminants, namely metronidazole and paracetamol, under visible light and sunlight, attaining 99 % degradation within 100–180 min under optimum circumstances. Critical variables were methodically assessed to ascertain optimal photocatalytic efficacy, including pollutant concentration, catalyst dosage, pH, and light intensity. Mechanistic insights identified hydroxyl radicals as the predominant active species, augmented by superoxide anions and photogenerated holes. The composite demonstrated remarkable durability and reusability across five deterioration cycles, with negligible performance decline. Moreover, its utilization in actual water matrices, such as lake water and tap water, validated its resilience and adaptability, attaining uniform degradation rates under diverse environmental situations. The results identify Ti3C2MXene/CDs/ZnO as an effective photocatalyst for the eco-friendly remediation of pharmaceutical pollutants in wastewater, aiding in the reduction of environmental contamination and tackling public health issues related to antibiotic and medication residues.

Phongsakorn Kantang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Aphinya Thinthasit: Investigation, Formal analysis. Indra Memdi Khoris: Supervision, Methodology, Investigation. David Nugroho: Supervision, Methodology, Investigation. Jaebeom Lee: Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.
